title: Day 1 · Phase 1 — Re-anchoring: heat is energy, but it's not temperature
bodyHtml:
DAY 1 · Phase 1 — Picking up the heat bridge

Unit 4 said "energy lost to friction becomes heat." Unit 5 quantifies the heat — and untangles heat from temperature.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS (preview)

K1. Distinguish thermal energy, temperature, and heat. R3. Why atmospheric entry GLOWS — preview to Day 10.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Where we are: end of Unit 4. We have impact KE in TNT equivalents. The damage isn't just shockwave — it's HEAT.

Today's pivot: Two payoffs in Unit 5. (1) atmospheric entry — Day 10 explains why most asteroids burn up before they hit. (2) impact thermal damage — Day 11 explains the fireball and thermal radiation that does damage even outside the crater zone.

Lesson Flow

(~50 min; adjust for block.)

TimeWhat happens
0–5Return Unit 4 transfer tasks. Highlight strong yield-conversion work. "Energy was the damage answer. But the damage MECHANISM is heat. Today we untangle heat from temperature."
5–18Demo: a thimble of boiling water (100 °C). A bathtub of warm bathwater (40 °C). Which has more thermal energy? Force the room to debate. (Answer: bathtub — vastly more molecules, even though cooler.) Same DEMO: a single match flame (very hot) vs. a campfire (less hot per unit volume but much more total energy).
18–28Notice & Wonder. Push toward the language distinction: temperature = how hot per molecule (intensive). Thermal energy = total molecular KE (extensive). Heat = thermal energy TRANSFERRED.
28–42Mini-lecture preview: Unit 5's three big landings. (1) calorimetry lab Day 5. (2) entropy + heat-engine efficiency on Days 8–9 (why perpetual motion machines fail). (3) atmospheric entry + impact heat on Days 10–11 — the asteroid payoff.
42–48Exit ticket: "Which contains MORE thermal energy: a cup of hot coffee at 80 °C, or a cold lake at 5 °C? Which is at HIGHER temperature? Why are these different questions?"
48–50Marzano on K1 preview.

Prep: Unit 4 transfer tasks scored; thermometer for prop; cup + a comparison image of a bathtub; match prop (don't light) or video.

Talk moves on temperature vs. thermal energy: when students conflate them, push: "If I have two glasses at the same temperature but one twice as much water — same thermal energy or different? Why?"

Differentiation: ELs — three-column glossary (heat / temperatura / calor — each defined visually); SWDs — verbal version of exit ticket offered; advanced — "if I combined the hot coffee with the cold lake (no other losses), what final temperature?"

title: Day 2 · Phase 1 — Specific heat: Q = mcΔT
bodyHtml:
DAY 2 · Phase 1 — How much heat to change temperature

Different materials need different amounts of energy per kg per °C. Water is the outlier (huge c).

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K2. Specific heat. S1. Compute Q = mcΔT.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Asteroids are mostly rock (c ≈ 800 J/kg·°C). Oceans are water (c ≈ 4186). When 2026-XJ heats up during atmospheric entry, the SAME energy raises rock temperature 5× more than it would water.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Hand back exit tickets. Anchor: "Heat and temperature are connected by a constant called specific heat."
5–18Direct instruction: Q = m·c·ΔT. Q = heat (J). m = mass (kg). c = specific heat (J/kg·°C). ΔT = temperature change (°C). Walk through each piece. Show the specific heat table — water c = 4186, aluminum 900, copper 387, ice 2090, rock 800.
18–32Worked example sequence: (1) How much heat to raise 0.5 kg of water by 20 °C? (2) Same energy applied to 0.5 kg of aluminum — what's the temperature rise? Force the room to see WHY water heats slowly.
32–42Group practice — 5 problems mixing materials, masses, and ΔT. Include one where the unknown is c (so they solve for material identification).
42–48Exit ticket: "How much heat is needed to raise 2 kg of copper from 20 °C to 80 °C?"
48–50Marzano on K2/S1.

Prep: specific-heat table printed; packet practice; calculator availability.

Talk moves on water's anomaly: push: "Water has c that's ~5× rock and ~10× metals. Why does it take so long to heat a pot of water? And why are oceans the planet's thermostat?"

Differentiation: ELs — equation with each piece labeled in words; SWDs — pre-built table for the worked examples; advanced — "if you heat 100 g of aluminum and 100 g of copper with the same 500 J, which gets hotter?"

title: Day 3 · Phase 2 — Heat transfer: conduction, convection, radiation
bodyHtml:
DAY 3 · Phase 2 — Three modes of heat moving

Conduction — touch. Convection — fluid currents. Radiation — light.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K3. Distinguish the three heat-transfer modes. Identify each in real scenarios.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Atmospheric entry heat moves THREE ways: friction (conduction at the asteroid surface), convection (heated air swept around it), and radiation (the asteroid GLOWS — and energy escapes as light). All three lose energy from the asteroid.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Hook: "Hot soup. How does it cool down?" Get students to name multiple paths.
5–22Direct instruction with demos. Conduction: hold a metal spoon in hot water — the handle warms (direct molecular contact). Convection: watch dye in a heated beaker — rises with the warmer (less dense) fluid. Radiation: hold hand near a hot mug WITHOUT TOUCHING — you feel infrared heat at distance (no medium needed).
22–35Classify activity: 8 real-world heat scenarios on a worksheet. Sun warming Earth. Hot drink in your hand. Convection oven. Wind chill. Asteroid glowing during entry. Microwave. House insulation. Pair-share answers.
35–43Class compilation: name each scenario by mode. Discuss disagreements.
43–48Exit ticket: "You see a glowing meteor cross the sky. By which mode does the meteor lose energy to YOU on the ground?" (Radiation only — vacuum/air between.)
48–50Marzano on K3.

Prep: spoon + hot water beaker; food-color dye + warm-water beaker for convection; hot mug; classify-the-scenario worksheet.

Talk moves on radiation vs. conduction: when students conflate them, push: "Does the mug touch your hand for radiation to work? What does that tell you?"

Differentiation: ELs — three-card visual (conduction / convection / radiation) with one example each; SWDs — fewer scenarios, more partner time; advanced — "why does a thermos use a vacuum gap? What modes does that defeat?"

title: Day 4 · Phase 2 — Phase changes + latent heat
bodyHtml:
DAY 4 · Phase 2 — Energy without temperature change

Q = mL. Phase changes happen at constant T while energy goes into breaking bonds.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K4. Phase changes + latent heat. S2. Compute Q = mL.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

2026-XJ doesn't just heat up at impact — it MELTS, then VAPORIZES. Each transition takes enormous energy. The crater's heat doesn't just raise rock temperature, it phases rock into vapor.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Hook: "Put a thermometer in a pot of boiling water. What temperature reads? It keeps boiling for 10 more minutes — does the temperature change?" (No — 100 °C.) Where does the heat go?
5–18Direct instruction: phase changes happen at constant T. Energy goes into breaking molecular bonds, not raising temperature. Latent heat L = energy per kg to phase-change. L_fusion (melt/freeze). L_vaporization (boil/condense). Water: L_f = 334 kJ/kg, L_v = 2260 kJ/kg.
18–30Worked example: how much heat to (a) raise 0.5 kg of ice from -20 °C to 0 °C — Q = mcΔT with c_ice. (b) MELT all that ice at 0 °C — Q = mL_f. (c) Raise water from 0 °C to 100 °C — Q = mcΔT with c_water. (d) BOIL all the water at 100 °C — Q = mL_v. Add them up — the latent steps DOMINATE.
30–42Plot temperature vs. heat for water — flat segments at the phase changes, sloped between. Show the canonical "heating curve."
42–48Exit ticket: "How much heat to vaporize 0.2 kg of water at 100 °C?"
48–50Marzano on K4/S2.

Prep: Latent-heat table (water + a few others); heating-curve diagram for the board; packet practice.

Talk moves on the latent-heat scale: when students see L_v = 2260 kJ/kg for water, push: "Compare to raising water 80 °C: only 334 kJ/kg. Boiling is more energy-intensive than ALL the heating. Why does sweating cool you?"

Differentiation: ELs — heating-curve diagram with each segment labeled in words; SWDs — multi-step worksheet with each Q calculation pre-numbered; advanced — "compute the total heat to convert 1 kg of ice at -10 °C to steam at 110 °C."

title: Day 5 · Phase 2 — Vernier calorimetry lab
bodyHtml:
DAY 5 · Phase 2 — Mixing hot and cold; measuring specific heat

Mix water at two temperatures. Predict final temperature using Q_lost = Q_gained. Then measure with Vernier temp probe.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S4. Vernier calorimetry. K2. Confirm specific-heat math.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If we confirm Q = mcΔT works in our lab cups, we can use it on planetary scales for the impact heat calculations on Day 11.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Lab procedure overview + safety beat (hot water, gloves, careful pouring).
5–15Teacher demo: 100 mL water at 70 °C mixed with 100 mL at 20 °C. Predict T_final using m_h·c·(T_h - T_f) = m_c·c·(T_f - T_c). Then measure with probe.
15–35Lab: pairs run 3 trials varying masses + temperatures. Predict T_f, measure with Vernier temp probe, compute % difference. Then: drop a hot metal piece into cold water, use Q_metal = -Q_water to compute the metal's specific heat (identifies unknown material).
35–43Class data: average specific heat for the unknown metal. Compare to known c values, identify material.
43–48Lab write-up start.
48–50Marzano on S4.

Prep: Vernier temperature probes + LabQuest; styrofoam cups (insulated calorimeters); hot plate + beakers of hot water; ice; balance; known metal pieces (aluminum + copper) labeled "unknown A / unknown B"; lab write-up template.

Talk moves on % difference: if a pair is 5% off, push: "What ENERGY is leaking out of your calorimeter? Where would it go?"

Differentiation: ELs — pair with a partner familiar with LoggerPro; SWDs — pre-built data table; advanced — "compute the heat lost to the cup itself using the cup's mass and specific heat. Does that close the gap?"

title: Day 6 · Phase 3 — Kinetic theory: temperature is molecular KE
bodyHtml:
DAY 6 · Phase 3 — What temperature IS, at the molecular level

Temperature ∝ average kinetic energy of molecules. Higher T = faster jiggling.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K1 (extended). Temperature as molecular KE. Distinguish from thermal energy (total) again.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

When the asteroid impact vaporizes rock, what's happening at the molecular level? Molecules gain enough KE to break free from solid → liquid → gas. Kinetic theory connects the energy framework to the molecular picture.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Hook: "You added energy yesterday and got a temperature change. Where did that energy GO inside the water?"
5–18Direct instruction: kinetic theory of gases. Molecules jiggle randomly. Temperature is proportional to the AVERAGE KE per molecule: KE_avg = (3/2)kT (k = Boltzmann constant). Higher T → faster avg molecular speed.
18–30Phase change as bond-breaking: in a solid, molecules vibrate in place. As T rises, KE grows. At melting point, KE is enough to break bonds → liquid. At boiling, even more → gas. The LATENT heat from Day 4 goes into bond-breaking, not avg KE.
30–40PhET States of Matter sim: heat solid argon. Watch molecular motion increase. See phase transitions.
40–48Exit ticket: "Why does temperature stay constant during a phase change, even though heat is being added?"
48–50Marzano on K1 extended.

Prep: PhET States of Matter loaded on lab computers; molecular-motion visualization for the board.

Talk moves on the constant-T-during-phase-change explanation: push: "Where is the heat going if not into avg KE? What's it doing to the molecules?" Lead to bond-breaking.

Differentiation: ELs — visual showing molecular vibration intensity vs. T; SWDs — fill-in answer template for exit ticket; advanced — "if T doubles (Kelvin), what happens to avg molecular speed? Hint: KE = ½mv²."

title: Day 7 · Phase 4 — First law of thermodynamics: ΔU = Q − W
bodyHtml:
DAY 7 · Phase 4 — Energy conservation for thermal systems

Change in internal energy = heat in MINUS work out. Energy conservation, written for gases.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K5. First law of thermodynamics.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The expanding fireball after impact is a thermodynamic system — gas at high T expanding against the surrounding atmosphere. The first law describes how its thermal energy converts to work pushing on the air (shockwave).

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Recap: energy conservation (Unit 4). Heat is a form of energy (Day 1).
5–18Direct instruction: ΔU = Q − W. ΔU = change in internal energy (thermal energy of the system). Q = heat ADDED to the system. W = work DONE BY the system on surroundings. The minus sign matters.
18–30Worked example 1: 500 J heat added to a gas. The gas does 200 J of work expanding. ΔU = 500 − 200 = 300 J. Internal energy rises.
30–40Worked example 2: a gas is compressed — 300 J of work is done ON it. No heat exchanged (adiabatic). W = -300 (gas did negative work). ΔU = 0 − (-300) = 300 J. Compression HEATS the gas. (This is why a bike pump gets warm.)
40–48Exit ticket: "100 J heat enters a system. The system does 150 J of work. What's ΔU?" (-50 J — internal energy DECREASES.)
48–50Marzano on K5.

Prep: bike pump for compression demo; packet practice; sign-convention chart (Q positive = heat in; W positive = work BY system).

Talk moves on the sign convention: students will mix up signs. Stop on every problem: "Is heat going IN or OUT? Is work being done BY or ON the system?"

Differentiation: ELs — sign-chart card; SWDs — pre-filled Q and W signs on worksheet; advanced — "how does the first law apply to a refrigerator? What's the sign of Q?"

title: Day 8 · Phase 4 — Second law: heat flows hot → cold; entropy
bodyHtml:
DAY 8 · Phase 4 — The direction of natural processes

Heat NEVER spontaneously flows cold to hot. Entropy of the universe always increases.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K6. Second law of thermodynamics + entropy direction. R1. Reason about which processes are spontaneous.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The asteroid's KE at impact becomes thermal energy — that's a HIGH-entropy state. There's no natural process that converts the heat back to organized motion. The impact is IRREVERSIBLE in this sense.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Hook: "Have you ever seen a broken glass un-shatter? Have you ever seen heat spontaneously flow from cold to hot? Why not?"
5–20Direct instruction: Second law (Clausius form): heat flows naturally from HOT to COLD, never the reverse, without external work. Entropy is a measure of disorder. Spontaneous processes increase entropy of the universe.
20–32Examples: melting ice (water has higher entropy than ice — molecules can move more). Diffusion of gas. Mixing hot + cold water (entropy higher than separated). The arrow of time is the arrow of entropy.
32–42Group discussion: classify 6 scenarios as entropy-INCREASING (spontaneous) or entropy-decreasing (requires energy input). Egg breaking. Refrigerator cooling food. Sun warming Earth. Battery being charged. Iron rusting. Water freezing in a freezer.
42–48Exit ticket: "Why can't you cool your kitchen by leaving the refrigerator door open?" (Because the heat dumped to the back of the fridge exceeds the heat removed from inside — net heating.)
48–50Marzano on K6/R1.

Prep: packet practice; entropy classification worksheet.

Talk moves on "fridge with door open": when students reach for "it would cool," push: "Where does the heat removed from inside the fridge GO? Compare to the cooling effect on the room."

Differentiation: ELs — "hot → cold" visual arrows; SWDs — partner discussion on each scenario; advanced — "could a perpetual motion machine exist that doesn't violate the FIRST law but does violate the SECOND? Why does the second law forbid it?"

title: Day 9 · Phase 4 — Heat engines + efficiency
bodyHtml:
DAY 9 · Phase 4 — Why 100% efficiency is impossible

Engines take heat from hot source, do work, dump waste heat to cold sink. Efficiency = W/Q_hot.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K7. Heat engine cycle + efficiency. S3. Compute efficiency.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The expanding fireball after impact is a NATURAL heat engine — it converts some of its thermal energy to mechanical work (the shockwave). But it can't convert 100% — the second law forbids it.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Quick check: second law direction.
5–18Direct instruction: heat engines move heat from HOT to COLD and extract some as WORK along the way. Efficiency = W/Q_hot = 1 − Q_cold/Q_hot. Always < 1. The second law forces SOME heat to be wasted.
18–32Worked examples: a power plant gets 1000 J from steam at 600 K, does 400 J of work, dumps 600 J to cold sink at 300 K. Efficiency = 40%. Compare to ideal Carnot efficiency: 1 − T_cold/T_hot = 1 − 300/600 = 50%. Real engines are less efficient than the Carnot LIMIT.
32–42Group practice — 4 problems. Compute real and Carnot efficiencies. Compare typical engines: car (~25%), power plant (~40%), human body (~25%).
42–48Exit ticket: "A car engine receives 1500 J of heat per cycle and produces 400 J of work. What's its efficiency?"
48–50Marzano on K7/S3.

Prep: packet practice; engine efficiency reference chart; calculator availability.

Talk moves on the Carnot LIMIT: push: "This is the MOST efficient engine that could possibly exist between these temperatures. Real engines are below this. Why is the limit set by temperatures alone?"

Differentiation: ELs — efficiency equation card with each piece labeled; SWDs — pre-built worksheet (Q_hot, W, Q_cold columns); advanced — "derive Carnot efficiency from second law principles."

title: Day 10 · Phase 5 — Atmospheric entry: why most asteroids burn up
bodyHtml:
DAY 10 · Phase 5 — Drag → heat → glow → ablation

Most space rocks NEVER reach the ground. Atmosphere does negative work, all of it becomes heat.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R3. Reason about atmospheric entry thermodynamics.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If 2026-XJ is small enough, ALL its KE goes into atmospheric heating before it touches the ground. If it's big enough to survive, only PART burns away. Today: what's the size threshold?

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Hook: a shooting star. What you're seeing is a sand-grain-to-pebble-sized rock burning up. Most of its mass becomes vapor before it reaches the ground.
5–18Mini-lecture: atmospheric drag is a friction-like force. At 15 km/s, drag is ENORMOUS. The work done by drag = ½ρ·v²·A·d. All of that becomes thermal energy at the surface of the rock.
18–32Worked example: a 1-kg pebble at 15 km/s. Its KE = ½·1·(15000)² = 1.1×10⁸ J. To vaporize all 1 kg of rock requires ~10⁷ J (using rock c + L_v). So all its KE × about 10 is available — the pebble TOTALLY vaporizes. Now repeat for a 10-kg rock — most still vaporizes but some core may survive. For 1000 kg, maybe 10% mass loss. For 10⁹ kg (like 2026-XJ), atmospheric losses are negligible — it reaches ground at nearly full mass.
32–42Class discussion: why does this matter for planetary defense? Small rocks are atmospheric problem (Chelyabinsk: airburst at 30 km, no crater, glass-shattering shockwave but no impact). Big rocks are ground problem.
42–48Exit ticket: "Why does a 1-kg rock burn up but a 10⁹-kg asteroid reach the ground? Reason in terms of surface area to mass ratio."
48–50Marzano on R3.

Prep: Chelyabinsk 2013 video or photo (the dashcam reels are iconic); atmospheric density reference; calculator.

Talk moves on the size threshold: push: "Surface-area-to-mass scales as 1/r. Small rocks have lots of surface area per unit mass — lots of drag per unit of KE. Big rocks have little. Why is THIS the deciding factor?"

Differentiation: ELs — visual showing different-sized rocks with different drag-to-mass ratios; SWDs — pre-built KE-vs-vaporization-energy worksheet for 4 sizes; advanced — "what's the largest rock that completely burns up in our atmosphere? Estimate."

title: Day 11 · Phase 5 — Asteroid impact thermal effects
bodyHtml:
DAY 11 · Phase 5 — The unit's payoff day

If 2026-XJ reaches ground, what's the thermal damage radius? Fireball, ignition, thermal radiation.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R3. Apply thermal framework to predict impact damage modes.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

2026-XJ at impact: its KE (computed Unit 4 Day 19) converts almost entirely to thermal energy. We trace where that energy goes — vaporized rock, fireball, thermal radiation pulse, ignition radius. The unit pays off.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Frame the day. "We have the impact KE in TNT. Today we trace where that energy goes thermally."
5–18Group calculation: given 2026-XJ's impact KE in Joules, partition it. ~30% goes to vaporizing rock (use Q = mcΔT + mL_v with crater mass). ~30% becomes shockwave kinetic energy in atmosphere. ~30% becomes THERMAL RADIATION (fireball glow + thermal pulse).
18–32Thermal radiation damage radius: the fireball emits like a blackbody. Energy deposited per m² at distance r = E_radiated / (4πr²). Use thresholds: 0.4 cal/cm² ignites paper. 5 cal/cm² causes third-degree burns. Compute distance for each.
32–42Compilation: build a damage-by-distance map. Within radius R₁ — total vaporization (crater zone). R₂ — total destruction by shockwave. R₃ — wildfire ignition (thermal). R₄ — third-degree burns. Beyond R₄ — felt but not catastrophic.
42–48Discussion: which damage mode reaches FURTHEST from impact? (Usually thermal — light travels far.)
48–50Marzano on R3.

Prep: 2026-XJ KE from Unit 4 Day 19; thermal damage threshold reference; calculator; whiteboard space for damage map.

Talk moves on partitioning: push: "How would you EXPERIMENTALLY measure which fraction goes where? Could you find that from a real crater?"

Differentiation: ELs — damage-by-distance table with three radii pre-labeled; SWDs — pre-built calculation worksheet; advanced — "estimate the thermal radiation damage radius for Tunguska's 15-megaton airburst."

title: Day 12 · Phase 5 — Unit 5 transfer task
bodyHtml:
DAY 12 · Phase 5 — Assessment of learning

4 multi-part problems integrating Q = mcΔT, phase changes, first law, second law / efficiency, and asteroid thermal.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

All Unit 5 targets, surfaced through transfer problems.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The transfer task includes one atmospheric-entry problem (R3) — given a small asteroid's mass + speed, predict whether it survives the atmosphere — as the load-bearing Reasoning prompt.

Lesson Flow

TimeWhat happens
0–5Brief reminder of the 4-dimension rubric (Identify / Calculate / Construct / Transfer). Lowest-dimension grading.
5–45Transfer task. Independent. 4 multi-part problems: (1) calorimetry — mix two known water samples + verify final temperature; (2) phase change — total heat to convert ice at -10 °C to steam at 110 °C; (3) heat engine — given Q_hot, Q_cold, compute W and efficiency, compare to Carnot; (4) asteroid atmospheric entry — given a pebble's mass + speed + needed vaporization energy, does it reach the ground?
45–48Final Marzano on the unit target list.
48–50Growth chart updated. Unit 6 begins tomorrow — waves are the LAST broad framework before the year's electricity unit.

Prep: transfer task printed; calculators; constants reference card (c, L, k); quiet testing environment; growth chart pages.

Differentiation on the transfer task: ELs — vocabulary glossary attached (heat, temperature, latent heat, entropy, efficiency); SWDs — option to verbal-explain dimension 4 (Transfer) of any problem; advanced — bonus prompt: "Use Unit 5 thinking to estimate the SMALLEST asteroid that would partially survive atmospheric entry."

Closing: Growth chart picked up tomorrow at start of Unit 6 — waves, sound, light.
